disease.name: Hyperthyroidism and Hypothyroidism
disease.note: Thyroid disorders encompass hyperthyroidism (Graves disease most common cause of overt hyperthyroidism; subclinical hyperthyroidism defined as low TSH with normal FT4/FT3) and hypothyroidism (Hashimoto thyroiditis most common cause in iodine-sufficient regions). TSH is the first-line screening test. Antithyroid antibody testing confirms autoimmune etiology. Methimazole preferred over PTU for Graves except in first trimester of pregnancy.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: If thyroid storm or myxedema coma -> EMERGENCY; if TSH <0.01 -> SEVERE; if TSH 0.01-0.4 subclinical -> MODERATE; if TSH normal -> MILD/treated

subtypes.name: Graves Disease (Overt Hyperthyroidism)
subtypes.management: Methimazole (MMI) 5-40mg/day based on FT4 level. For severe: MMI 30-40mg/day, then taper to 5-10mg maintenance. Beta-blocker (propranolol 20-40mg TID-QID or atenolol 25-100mg daily) for symptom control. Definitive therapy: RAI-131 or thyroidectomy if relapse after 12-18mo MMI. RAI contraindicated in active ophthalmopathy. Monitor TSH/FT4 q4-6 weeks during titration.

subtypes.name: Hashimoto Thyroiditis (Overt Hypothyroidism)
subtypes.management: Levothyroxine (LT4) monotherapy. Starting dose: 1.6 mcg/kg actual body weight. Healthy adults: start 50-75 mcg/day. If age >60 or CAD: start 12.5-25 mcg/day. Titrate by 12.5-25 mcg q4-6 weeks until TSH goal (0.5-2.5). Take 30-60min before breakfast, away from Ca/Fe/PPI. Monitor TSH q6 weeks during titration, then annually.

subtypes.name: Subclinical Hyperthyroidism and Hypothyroidism
subtypes.management: Subclinical hyperthyroidism: treat if TSH <0.1 (high risk AFib, bone loss) or if TSH 0.1-0.4 with risk factors. Low-dose MMI 5-10mg or RAI. Subclinical hypothyroidism: treat if TSH >10, or TSH 4.5-10 with TPO+ or symptoms or pregnancy or high CV risk. LT4 25-50 mcg/day. Reassess in 3-6 months.

subtypes.name: Thyroid Storm (Life-Threatening Thyrotoxicosis)
subtypes.management: Beta-blocker: propranolol 60-80mg PO q4h or esmolol IV. Thionamide: PTU 500-1000mg load then 250mg q4h OR MMI 20-25mg q4h. Iodine solution (SSKI 5 drops q6h or Lugol 4-8 drops q6h) given 1h after thionamide. Corticosteroid: hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h. Supportive care: cooling, IV fluids, treat precipitant. ICU monitoring.

bedside.name: Clinical score for thyroid storm (BWPS or Burch-Wartofsky)

laboratory.name: TSH (high-sensitivity third generation)
laboratory.indication: first-line screening for all thyroid disorders

laboratory.name: Complete blood count and LFTs before starting thionamides

imaging.name: Radioiodine uptake and scan (I-123 or Tc-99m)
imaging.indication: differentiate Graves (diffuse high uptake) vs thyroiditis (low uptake)

imaging.name: Thyroid scintigraphy with I-123
imaging.indication: toxic nodule, hot vs cold nodule

management.protocolReference: ATA Guidelines for Hyperthyroidism (2016), ATA Guidelines for Hypothyroidism (2014), Thyroid Storm Management Protocol (Burch-Wartofsky Score)

pathways.criteria: TSH <0.01, elevated FT4/FT3, TSI+, diffuse goiter
pathways.treatment: Start MMI 15-40mg/day based on severity. Beta-blocker (propranolol 40-80mg TID or atenolol 50mg daily) until euthyroid. Check CBC and LFTs baseline. Monitor TSH/FT4 q4-6 weeks. Taper MMI to maintenance 5-10mg. Consider definitive therapy (RAI or surgery) after 12-18 months if not in remission.

pathways.criteria: TSH >10 with low FT4, or TSH 4.5-10 with TPO+ and symptoms
pathways.treatment: LT4 1.6 mcg/kg/day. For healthy <60: start 50-75 mcg. For elderly/CAD: start 12.5-25 mcg, titrate slowly. Recheck TSH in 6 weeks, adjust by 12.5-25 mcg until TSH 0.5-2.5. Once stable, check TSH annually. Avoid interference: Ca, Fe, PPIs, fiber separate by 4h.

pathways.criteria: Burch-Wartofsky score >=45, or any point with high suspicion
pathways.treatment: ICU admission. Propranolol 60-80mg PO q4h or esmolol IV loading + infusion. PTU 500-1000mg loading then 250mg q4h (or MMI 20-25mg q4h). 1h later: SSKI 5 drops q6h or Lugol 4-8 drops q6h. Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h. Active cooling, IV fluids. Treat precipitating cause. Plasmapheresis if refractory.
